title: Geoscientists

infobox.value: is a [scientist](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") who studies the structure, composition, and [history](/wiki/History_of_Earth "History of Earth") of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"). Geologists incorporate techniques from [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), and [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography") to perform [research](/wiki/Research "Research") in the [field](/wiki/Field_research "Field research") and the [laboratory](/wiki/Laboratory "Laboratory"). Geologists work in the [energy](/wiki/Energy_industry "Energy industry") and [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") sectors to exploit [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource"). They monitor environmental hazards such as [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake"), [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"), [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") and [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide"). Geologists are also important contributors to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") discussions.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Geoscientists](/w/index.php?title=Geoscientists&redirect=no "Geoscientists"))

Scientist who studies geology

For the member of [Animal Collective](/wiki/Animal_Collective "Animal Collective"), see [Geologist (musician)](/wiki/Geologist_(musician) "Geologist (musician)").

Geologist

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| *The Geologist* by [Carl Spitzweg](/wiki/Carl_Spitzweg "Carl Spitzweg"), c. 1860 | |
| Occupation | |
| Names | Geologist |
| Occupation type | [Profession](/wiki/Profession "Profession") |
| Activity sectors | Government [Mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") [Petroleum industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") [Engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") |
| Description | |
| Related jobs | [Seismologist](/wiki/Seismologist "Seismologist") [Volcanologist](/wiki/Volcanologist "Volcanologist") [Geophysicist](/wiki/Geophysicist "Geophysicist") [Geoscientists](/wiki/Geoscientists "Geoscientists") |

A **geologist** is a [scientist](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") who studies the structure, composition, and [history](/wiki/History_of_Earth "History of Earth") of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"). Geologists incorporate techniques from [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), and [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography") to perform [research](/wiki/Research "Research") in the [field](/wiki/Field_research "Field research") and the [laboratory](/wiki/Laboratory "Laboratory"). Geologists work in the [energy](/wiki/Energy_industry "Energy industry") and [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") sectors to exploit [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource"). They monitor environmental hazards such as [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake"), [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"), [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") and [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide"). Geologists are also important contributors to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") discussions.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geologist&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Hutton_James_portrait_Raeburn.jpg/250px-Hutton_James_portrait_Raeburn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hutton_James_portrait_Raeburn.jpg)

[Scotsman](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") James Hutton, father of modern geology

[James Hutton](/wiki/James_Hutton "James Hutton") is often viewed as the first modern geologist.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In 1785 he presented a paper entitled *Theory of the Earth* to the [Royal Society of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Royal Society of Edinburgh"). In his paper, he explained his theory that the Earth must be much older than had previously been supposed to allow enough time for mountains to be eroded and for [sediments](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment") to form new rocks at the bottom of the sea, which in turn were raised up to become dry land. Hutton published a two-volume version of his ideas in 1795 ([Vol. 1](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/12861), [Vol. 2](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/14179)).
Followers of Hutton were known as *[Plutonists](/wiki/Plutonism "Plutonism")* because they believed that some rocks were formed by *vulcanism*, which is the deposition of lava from volcanoes, as opposed to the *[Neptunists](/wiki/Neptunism "Neptunism")*, led by [Abraham Werner](/wiki/Abraham_Gottlob_Werner "Abraham Gottlob Werner"), who believed that all rocks had settled out of a large ocean whose level gradually dropped over time.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Geologists_at_work_-_NARA_-_517104.jpg/250px-Geologists_at_work_-_NARA_-_517104.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geologists_at_work_-_NARA_-_517104.jpg)

"Geologists at work" from the U.S. Geological and Geographic Survey of the Territories (1874 - 06/30/1879). Photographer: [William Henry Jackson](/wiki/William_Henry_Jackson "William Henry Jackson")

The first geological map of the United States was produced in 1809 by [William Maclure](/wiki/William_Maclure "William Maclure").[[2]](#cite_note-Maclure1817-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 1807, Maclure commenced the self-imposed task of making a geological survey of the United States. Almost every state in the Union was traversed and mapped by him; the [Allegheny Mountains](/wiki/Allegheny_Mountains "Allegheny Mountains") being crossed and recrossed some 50 times.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The results of his unaided labors were submitted to the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society") in a memoir entitled *Observations on the Geology of the United States explanatory of a Geological Map*, and published in the *Society's Transactions*, together with the nation's first geological map.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This antedates [William Smith](/wiki/William_Smith_(geologist) "William Smith (geologist)")'s geological map of England by six years, although it was constructed using a different classification of rocks.

[Sir Charles Lyell](/wiki/Sir_Charles_Lyell "Sir Charles Lyell") first published his famous book, *[Principles of Geology](/wiki/Principles_of_Geology "Principles of Geology")*,[[6]](#cite_note-6) in 1830. This book, which influenced the thought of [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), successfully promoted the doctrine of [uniformitarianism](/wiki/Uniformitarianism "Uniformitarianism"). This theory states that slow geological processes have occurred throughout the [Earth's history](/wiki/History_of_Earth "History of Earth") and are still occurring today. In contrast, [catastrophism](/wiki/Catastrophism "Catastrophism") is the theory that Earth's features formed in single, catastrophic events and remained unchanged thereafter. Though Hutton believed in uniformitarianism, the idea was not widely accepted at the time.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geologist&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Geology_explained%5E_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1450258.jpg/250px-Geology_explained%5E_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1450258.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geology_explained%5E_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1450258.jpg)

A young geologist learns about [flow banding](/wiki/Flow_banding "Flow banding")

For an aspiring geologist, training typically includes significant coursework in [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), and [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), in addition to classes offered through the geology department; [historical](/wiki/Historical_geology "Historical geology") and physical geology, igneous and metamorphic [petrology](/wiki/Petrology "Petrology") and petrography, [hydrogeology](/wiki/Hydrogeology "Hydrogeology"), [sedimentology](/wiki/Sedimentology "Sedimentology"), [stratigraphy](/wiki/Stratigraphy "Stratigraphy"), [mineralogy](/wiki/Mineralogy "Mineralogy"), [palaeontology](/wiki/Palaeontology "Palaeontology"), [physical geography](/wiki/Physical_geography "Physical geography") and [structural geology](/wiki/Structural_geology "Structural geology") are among the many required areas of study. Most geologists also need skills in [GIS](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system") and other [mapping](/wiki/Map "Map") techniques. Geology students often spend portions of the year, especially the summer though sometimes during a January term, living and working under field conditions with faculty members (often referred to as "field camp"). Many non-geologists often take geology courses or have expertise in geology that they find valuable to their fields; this is common in the fields of [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography"), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning"), [environmental studies](/wiki/Environmental_studies "Environmental studies"), among others.

### Specialization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geologist&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Specialization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/VNIIOarctic.jpg/250px-VNIIOarctic.jpg)](/wiki/File:VNIIOarctic.jpg)

A geologist working in the Arctic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/MakhteshGadolJurassicRidge.JPG/250px-MakhteshGadolJurassicRidge.JPG)](/wiki/File:MakhteshGadolJurassicRidge.JPG)

Geologists exploring [Jurassic](/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic") sedimentary rocks in [Makhtesh Gadol](/wiki/Makhtesh_Gadol "Makhtesh Gadol"), Negev Desert, Israel

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Tephrochronology_iceland.JPG/250px-Tephrochronology_iceland.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tephrochronology_iceland.JPG)

Geologist explaining the importance of volcanic ash layers to students on field in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland")

Geologists, can be generally identified as a specialist in one or more of the various geoscience disciplines, such as a geophysicist or geochemist. Geologists may concentrate their studies or research in one or more of the following disciplines:

* [Economic geology](/wiki/Economic_geology "Economic geology"): the study of [ore genesis](/wiki/Ore_genesis "Ore genesis"), and the mechanisms of ore creation, [geostatistics](/wiki/Geostatistics "Geostatistics").
* [Engineering geology](/wiki/Engineering_geology "Engineering geology"): application of the geologic sciences to engineering practice for the purpose of assuring that the geologic factors affecting the location, design, construction, operation and maintenance of engineering works are recognized and adequately provided for;
* [Geophysics](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics"): the applied branch deals with the application of physical methods such as gravity, seismicity, electricity, magnetic properties to study the earth.
* [Geochemistry](/wiki/Geochemistry "Geochemistry"): the applied branch deals with the study of the chemical makeup and behaviour of rocks, and the study of the behaviour of their minerals.
* [Geochronology](/wiki/Geochronology "Geochronology"): the study of isotope geology specifically toward determining the date within the past of rock formation, [metamorphism](/wiki/Metamorphism "Metamorphism"), mineralization and geological events (notably, [meteorite impacts](/wiki/Meteorite_impact "Meteorite impact")).
* [Geomorphology](/wiki/Geomorphology "Geomorphology"): the study of landforms and the processes that create them.
* [Hydrogeology](/wiki/Hydrogeology "Hydrogeology"): the study of the origin, occurrence and movement of groundwater water in a subsurface geological system.
* Igneous [petrology](/wiki/Petrology "Petrology"): the study of [igneous](/wiki/Igneous_rock "Igneous rock") processes such as [igneous differentiation](/wiki/Igneous_differentiation "Igneous differentiation"), [fractional crystallization](/wiki/Fractional_crystallization_(geology) "Fractional crystallization (geology)"), intrusive and volcanological phenomena.
* [Isotope geology](/wiki/Isotope_geology "Isotope geology"): the case of the isotopic composition of rocks to determine the processes of rock and planetary formation.
* [Metamorphic](/wiki/Metamorphic_rock "Metamorphic rock") petrology: the study of the effects of metamorphism on minerals and rocks.
* [Marine geology](/wiki/Marine_geology "Marine geology"): the study of the seafloor; involves geophysical, geochemical, sedimentological and paleontological investigations of the ocean floor and coastal margins. Marine geology has strong ties to physical oceanography and plate tectonics.
* [Mineralogy](/wiki/Mineralogy "Mineralogy"): the study of the chemistry, [crystal structure](/wiki/Crystallography "Crystallography"), and physical (including optical) properties of minerals and mineralized artifacts. Specific studies within mineralogy include the processes of mineral origin and formation, classification of minerals, their geographical distribution, as well as their utilization.
* [Palaeoclimatology](/wiki/Palaeoclimatology "Palaeoclimatology"): the application of geological science to determine the climatic conditions present in the Earth's atmosphere within the Earth's history.
* [Palaeontology](/wiki/Palaeontology "Palaeontology"): the classification and taxonomy of fossils within the geological record and the construction of a palaeontological history of the Earth.
* [Pedology](/wiki/Pedology_(soil_study) "Pedology (soil study)"): the study of soil, soil formation, and [regolith](/wiki/Regolith "Regolith") formation.
* [Petroleum geology](/wiki/Petroleum_geology "Petroleum geology"): the study of [sedimentary basins](/wiki/Sedimentary_basin "Sedimentary basin") applied to the search for [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbons "Hydrocarbons") ([oil exploration](/wiki/Oil_exploration "Oil exploration")).
* [Planetary geology](/wiki/Planetary_geology "Planetary geology"): the study of geosciences as it relates to other celestial bodies, namely [planets](/wiki/Planets "Planets") and their [moons](/wiki/Moons "Moons"). This includes the subdisciplines of lunar geology, [selenology](/wiki/Geology_of_the_Moon "Geology of the Moon"), and martian geology, [areology](/wiki/Areology "Areology").
* [Sedimentology](/wiki/Sedimentology "Sedimentology"): the study of [sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rocks "Sedimentary rocks"), [strata](/wiki/Stratum "Stratum"), formations, [eustasy](/wiki/Eustasy "Eustasy") and the processes of modern-day sedimentary and erosive systems.
* [Seismology](/wiki/Seismology "Seismology"): the study of [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquakes "Earthquakes").
* [Structural geology](/wiki/Structural_geology "Structural geology"): the study of [folds](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)"), [faults](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)"), [foliation](/wiki/Foliation_(geology) "Foliation (geology)") and [rock microstructure](/wiki/Rock_microstructure "Rock microstructure") to determine the deformational history of rocks and regions.
* [Volcanology](/wiki/Volcanology "Volcanology"): the study of [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"), their eruptions, [lavas](/wiki/Lava "Lava"), [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma") processes and hazards.

## Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geologist&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Employment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/CratersOfTheMoonFordTent.jpg/250px-CratersOfTheMoonFordTent.jpg)](/wiki/File:CratersOfTheMoonFordTent.jpg)

"Picturesque camp made by a lone geologist on the cinders of Inferno". This photo was taken during a U.S. Department of the Interior Geological Survey in 1921.

Professional geologists may work in the mining industry or in the associated area of [mineral exploration](/wiki/Mineral_exploration "Mineral exploration").[[7]](#cite_note-berkman-7) They may also work in oil and gas industry.[[7]](#cite_note-berkman-7)

Some geologists also work for a wide range of government agencies, private firms, and non-profit and academic institutions. They are usually hired on a contract basis or hold permanent positions within private firms or official agencies (such as the [Geological Survey and Mineral Exploration of Iran](/wiki/Geological_Survey_and_Mineral_Exploration_of_Iran "Geological Survey and Mineral Exploration of Iran")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Local, state, and national governments hire geologists to work on geological projects that are of interest to the public community. The investigation of a country's natural resources is often a key role when working for government institutions; the work of the geologist in this field can be made publicly available to help the community make more informed decisions related to the exploitation of resources, management of the environment and the safety of critical infrastructure - all of which is expected to bring greater wellbeing to the country.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This 'wellbeing' is often in the form of greater tax revenues from new or extended mining projects or through better infrastructure and/or natural disaster planning.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Mudlogging.JPG/250px-Mudlogging.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mudlogging.JPG)

Geologist [mud logging](/wiki/Mud_logging "Mud logging"), common in petroleum and water-well drilling

An [engineering geologist](/wiki/Engineering_geology "Engineering geology") is employed to investigate [geologic hazards](/wiki/Geologic_hazards "Geologic hazards") and geologic constraints for the planning, design and construction of public and private engineering projects, [forensic](/wiki/Forensic "Forensic") and post-mortem studies, and [environmental impact analysis](/wiki/Environmental_impact_analysis "Environmental impact analysis"). [Exploration geologists](/wiki/Exploration_geophysics "Exploration geophysics") use all aspects of geology and geophysics to locate and study natural resources. In many countries or U.S. states without specialized [environmental remediation](/wiki/Environmental_remediation "Environmental remediation") licensure programs, the environmental remediation field is often dominated by professional geologists, particularly hydrogeologists, with professional concentrations in this aspect of the field. [Petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") and [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") companies use [mudloggers](/wiki/Mud_logging "Mud logging"), and large-scale land developers use the skills of geologists and engineering geologists to help them locate oil and minerals, adapt to local features such as [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") topography or earthquake risk, and comply with environmental regulations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Geologists in academia usually hold an advanced degree in a specialized area within their geological discipline and are employed by universities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Professional designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geologist&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Professional designation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Geologists-tools_hg.jpg/250px-Geologists-tools_hg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geologists-tools_hg.jpg)

The [rock hammer](/wiki/Geologist%27s_hammer "Geologist's hammer") and [hand lens](/wiki/Magnifying_glass "Magnifying glass") (or [loupe](/wiki/Loupe "Loupe")) are two of the most characteristic tools carried by geologists in the field.

In Canada, [National Instrument 43-101](/wiki/National_Instrument_43-101 "National Instrument 43-101") requires reports containing estimates of mineral resources and reserves to be prepared by, or under the supervision of, a Qualified Person (QP) who has at least five years of experience with the reported minerals and is a member of a professional association. The QP accepts personal liability for the professional quality of the report and underlying work.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The rules and guidelines codified in National Instrument 43-101 were introduced after a scandal in 1997 where [Bre-X](/wiki/Bre-X "Bre-X") geologists [salted](/wiki/Salting_(confidence_trick) "Salting (confidence trick)") drill core samples at a gold exploration property in Busang, Indonesia. The falsified drilling results misled Bre-X investors and upon discovery of the fraud, the company collapsed in the largest gold mining scam in history.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") exists the professional title of EurGeol (European Geologist[[11]](#cite_note-11)) awarded by the [European Federation of Geologists](/w/index.php?title=European_Federation_of_Geologists&action=edit&redlink=1 "European Federation of Geologists (page does not exist)").

## Professional Societies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geologist&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Professional Societies")]

Main article: [List of geoscience organizations](/wiki/List_of_geoscience_organizations "List of geoscience organizations")

Geologists may belong to a number of [professional societies](/wiki/Professional_societies "Professional societies") promoting research, networking, and professional development within the field.

* [American Association of Petroleum Geologists](/wiki/American_Association_of_Petroleum_Geologists "American Association of Petroleum Geologists") (AAPG)
* [American Geosciences Institute](/wiki/American_Geosciences_Institute "American Geosciences Institute") (AGI)
* [American Geophysical Union](/wiki/American_Geophysical_Union "American Geophysical Union") (AGU)
* [European Association of Geoscientists and Engineers](/wiki/European_Association_of_Geoscientists_and_Engineers "European Association of Geoscientists and Engineers") (EAGE) – Professional organization for geoscientists and engineers
* [European Federation of Geologists](/w/index.php?title=European_Federation_of_Geologists&action=edit&redlink=1 "European Federation of Geologists (page does not exist)") (EFG)
* [European Geosciences Union](/wiki/European_Geosciences_Union "European Geosciences Union") (EGU) – International science society
* [Geological Society of America](/wiki/Geological_Society_of_America "Geological Society of America") (GSA)
* [British Geological Survey](/wiki/British_Geological_Survey "British Geological Survey") (BGS)
* [Geological Society of London](/wiki/Geological_Society_of_London "Geological Society of London") (GSL)
* [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") (USGS)
* [International Commission on Stratigraphy](/wiki/International_Commission_on_Stratigraphy "International Commission on Stratigraphy") (ICS)
* [International Union of Geological Sciences](/wiki/International_Union_of_Geological_Sciences "International Union of Geological Sciences") (IUGS)
* [Mineralogical Society of America](/wiki/Mineralogical_Society_of_America "Mineralogical Society of America") (MSA)
* [Society for Sedimentary Geology](/wiki/Society_for_Sedimentary_Geology "Society for Sedimentary Geology") (SEPM)
* [Society of Economic Geologists](/wiki/Society_of_Economic_Geologists "Society of Economic Geologists") (SEG)
